speaker: interviewer
text: Today is December 11, 1995. The survivor is David Jakubowski. And this is tape number 5.

speaker: interviewee
text: So as I mentioned at the end of the last tape, I took the rabbi, whom I met in Warsaw and then in the concentration camp of Budzyn, to the hospital. And I kept him there for three months [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] until the Poles from the camp started to talk that the Jewish doctor keeps a rabbi there for such a long time. So I let him-- I have to-- I had to let him go. He was reading-- I don't know where he found it-- the little chumash, a little booklet like this, every day and praying. I said, pray for me, too. And he said, don't worry. So-- but when I left, when I discharged him, there was no more work because the Americans were approaching. And it was very close to Pesach. The first Seder in the evening-- for the first Seder, he came to me. And from his memory, he read the whole-- he, I mean, recited the whole Haggadah. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, what are you going to eat? He says, I'm not going to eat bread. He said, Rabbi-- I said, Rabbi, if you're not going to eat a piece of bread, you're going to die. You're so weak. He said to me the unforgettable words, 'Everything is in the hands of the Almighty.'

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went to the kapo, to a friend of mine. I told him the story. I said, what, what I'm going to do with this rabbi? He said to me, give me the piece of bread, and I give you mashed potatoes. So the rabbi said, mashed potatoes I will eat. And that-- that's how we saved the rabbi's life.

speaker: interviewee
text: On the second Seder after he finished, he says to me again the unforgettable words. He said, [NON-ENGLISH]-- in Jewish-- next Seder we'll be free. And wherever we'll be, we'll meet for the first Seder, even at the end of the world. I was smiling because there were rumors that the whole camp will be finished. And he said to me, [NON-ENGLISH], he says. If I, Rabbi David Shapiro, tell you that we will survive, we will survive. And we did. And the first Seder, I spent with him in a town near Furth in Germany. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] It was somewhere in April-- around middle of April when the Americans started to approach. We heard even the artillery fire. And we're digging against the tanks, something to stop them. And an order came to move us all from Hersbruck to Dachau, south of Munich, the famous concentration camp, which was even there before the war. Now, we marched every-- daily. It was a few hundred kilometers-- daily, slept at night in the woods. The commanding officer was a new one. He was an officer of the first-- during the First World War. And he was a nice guy-- the last two months of our camp. And he gave an order not to shoot anybody who can't walk. Just get a few wagons with horses and put them there, and not to shoot anybody, which, as I know, it never happened in the history of concentration camps. Because who couldn't walk, they shot him.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we walked. And after a few days, we came to a village. The name of it was Hohenfels, H-O-H-E-N-F-A-- F-E-L-S, and south of big city, Regensburg, not Munich.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there, the commanding officer said, you stay here with the sick men. There were around 40, 42. Take them off the wagon. Puts them in the barns. He took two barns and left 10 SS soldiers-- and to wait for the Americans. And we are going to Dachau. When the main group left for Dachau, the soldiers immediately run away to the village and changed their clothes to civilian ones. We caught them later. Now we were free men. But we were clothed in the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] concentration camp blue and white. But I was walking in the town. And I went to the bakery, you know, and got bread. I said, I have to have bread. They were so scared of us-- unbelievable. Whatever I wanted, they gave me bread. They-- I wanted potatoes. They gave me sugar-- throw in everything, whatever I wanted. Now, interesting that every noon there was an American fighter plane above, above us, our heads. And we put our coats out that they know who we are. They knew. And they were showing us, go into the field. And every day, they were killing a cow for us. Now, the poor guys, they started to eat. And a lot-- and a few died of them because it was too much fat. But I forgot to-- forgot to say that I won't go. I said to my friend of mine that I won't go to Dachau. I'm going to stay here and hid. We hid in the straw and in the hay. And they left.

speaker: interviewee
text: So one day when I was walking into town, I heard, like, tanks coming. And there were rumors the Germans are coming back. So we hid in a-- in a hole.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I look out, and I see a Jeep with a white star on the hood and a-- and a black driver. I said to my friends, now, Germans, they're [GERMAN]. They're super race. They don't have black soldier, must be Americans. So I ran out. Oh my goodness, I was surrounded by the American boys. They were wonderful boys because these are the ones who liberated concentration camps. Never in my life I had so much chocolate and so much cigarettes. I remember Camels, Lucky Strikes. I was not a smoker. And then I even have a paper, a major of the-- of the battalion who liberated us, Major Robertson.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then came the commanding officer of the whole regiment, a lieutenant colonel. He asked, what do you-- what do you need? I said, I need two, two houses for my men-- sick men, and food, and everything in it.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, all right. But give me a few hours. I said, all right. A few hours later, he calls me. He said, here are your homes, two beautiful-- with food, linen. And there, I had my men. They transported them by ambulance. They transported them. And there-- from there-- this was end of April. The war was still going on. From Hohenfels, they moved us outside of the, the village. There were barracks of the SS. And we organized there a hospital for these people. And more, there were brought. They brought more in. Now, we didn't have any instrument. There were lot of-- but there was-- one was a tuberculosis specialist from Warsaw, a guy who needs machines for pneumothorax, and so on. I said, OK. Let me go.

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to Nuremberg. And I went to a store with, with equipment, medical equipment. And I had-- you know what? I was still on the stripes. What do you want? I said, this, this, this, and this. Five minutes I was there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then [LAUGHS] one day comes to me a captain of the army-- of the American Army. He was from Chicago, a Pole. He knew Polish. I didn't know English.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he tells-- told me in Polish, well, you know-- in the beginning, we need linen for the mattresses, for the pillows. Where do we get it? And I said, you come with me. I'll get it from you. We went to the village. And I went to the mayor of the town, Oberburgermeister, mayor of the town. And I said to him, you know who I am?

speaker: interviewee
text: I am a Jew. You Germans killed my mother, my father, my sister, and my family. You took everything away. I give you one hour, and I have to have this and this and this and this. He says, OK. You will have it. Just be quiet. You will have it. And you know in the-- in the small villages, there was a man who was in the middle of the market and ringing a bell. And in one hour, we have a full truck. We came to the full-- with a full truck there. And we had linen for the hospital.

speaker: interviewee
text: One week later, I got the, the captain, the Polish captain comes. And he says, I'm in big trouble. The commanding officer there and there of the whole region, an American, is calling me in-- probably what he did. I said, is that so? Let me go with you. So I went with him. He was a major, major of the American Army. And he was a military commander of the whole area. And I gave it to him. Boy! He says, we are here to teach them democracy. I said, whom, these killers and gangsters? You're going to kill-- you're going to teach them democracy? Forget it! Not this generation.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we need this and this. We needed this and this, and I had to get it. He said, please calm down. Do me a favor, he says to me, you know, behind the desk he was sitting. If you need anything, come to me. You will get anything what you wanted. But please come to me, and I'll get it for you. OK, fine. There, I saw the first game of baseball. The boys were playing. [LAUGHS] And he was teaching me, the captain. His name was Captain-- see, I remember-- Captain [? Shapiro ?] from Chicago. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And after a few weeks, they closed it. And they moved all the sick ones to Amberg. This was a Middle, Middle-Ages town which was-- city-- which was not touched by the American bombers because it was a beautiful city. And I was in charge of one hospital for people to recuperate from the concentration camps and from the POWs. And I was also-- there was a Jewish community there, a small Jewish community, but only from DPs, displaced person, and a rabbi there. And I became the, the physician, the doctor of the Jewish community. And after a few months, the Americans decided they have to have a big hospital for displaced person tuberculosis patients. So we organized a 500-bed hospital. And for the time, I was director of the hospital-- 500-bed tuberculosis hospital. Then another one came in. I was the second in charge because I was-- started to work also for the UNRRA-- you know what UNRRA is-- and the IRO, International Refugee Organization.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I was busy. I had my Jeep, my driver. And I had to travel around the camps, medical supervision.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Now-- but I had-- I could do very sick tuberculosis patient, and especially Jewish patients-- to send to Switzerland. They were-- They were sent there by the American Joint Distribution Committee. So I sent quite a few for a few months to recuperate to Switzerland. We did there everything-- pneumothoraxes. But we couldn't do surgeries-- big surgeries. This, I was sending to university hospital in Germany, a big professor there. I forgot his name, who discovered this operation. He was doing this. There in Amberg, I met my wife. And we got married in Amberg on August 18, 1946. Naturally, the whole American community was there. There were quite a few American Jewish soldiers there with their wives. Because she-- before we got married, she was teaching English in the-- in the Jewish school.

speaker: interviewee
text: So everybody was there. And then we had a big dinner. She didn't know how to cook. This doesn't matter. But we had a girl.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then IRO took me over. And I had to visit the Displaced Person camp as a physician. They were all under my supervision between Munich and Amberg. It was quite an area, quite a few Displaced Person camps. And I-- and I checked them for tuberculosis. I had a Roentgen machine, x-ray machine, which I took away from the Germans, a portable x-ray machine. And then I was x-raying all the inmates of the Displaced Person. And those who had tuberculosis were left-- sent to the sanatorium. Those who didn't, were healthy, were able to, to go to Israel or any other country. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So-- and then we left there. And we went to Regensburg from Amberg. And I was employed there again for the camps by the Palestine Jewish organization, I think they called them, or something like this. I was very active through all the four years. I, I worked for them for the last two years, from '47 to '49. We were living in Regensburg. I had my apartment or a house. It was easy living. The Germans, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they said no. They loved to say that it was a hoax, the whole-- the whole Holocaust. They couldn't believe me, everybody said. I was-- I didn't know anything about it. They lived next to the concentration camps. They saw us in Hersbruck.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you talk with them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Huh?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you talk with them?

speaker: interviewee
text: After the war, not during the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, during-- after the war, you--

speaker: interviewee
text: After the war, sure. Germany was a free Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: I know. But you talked to particular people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. I talked to the Germans. But everyone said, I didn't know anything about it. Nobody knew anything! [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So-- but we convinced them and tried to convince them what happened there. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And in-- I got a visa from the American Joint Distribution Committee. They gave me a visa. I was supposed to go to Israel because I had a cousin in Israel. But they said to my wife, you are not the type for Israel. You better go to-- come to America. And they gave me the visa, the first. There were 50 visas. I got number one because I was very active during the whole four years. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] May 30, 1949, I came to the United States, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the blessed country. I didn't know a word of English. I went to make a telephone call, and I took the money. I asked them, what is the dime, and what is a nickel? Because it was a dime, the telephone call. [LAUGHS] She knew English. And I went to, to a-- not to a residency, but I became a-- see, you forget? Before the residency. Internship.

speaker: interviewee
text: Internship in a hospital, Coney Island Hospital in Brooklyn, New York. I was there for one year. It was difficult for me because I didn't know any English, and I had to write histories. So my wife helped me. But after three months, I knew English because there was a-- an American boy who studied in Vienna. And he said, there is only one way you can learn English-- is watch the television but only-- no singing, but the cowboy shows where they talk. And that's how I learned. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And it wasn't that easy there, but I was already-- I mean, I have the-- we had the rotating internship. I was three months in obstetrics. And for me, there was nothing. I mean, the, the, the chief of obstetrics, she left me alone. I was delivering the kids, tens of them because she was trusting me. I told you my history.

speaker: interviewee
text: So it was in summer of 1950. There was a general-- a general, retired general from Pennsylvania came to our hospital and tried to get a few physician to work in a tuberculosis hospital in Pennsylvania, in South Mountain near Gettysburg. So naturally, I went, and I told him my experiences. When he heard this, boy, he grabbed me immediately. He said, you had-- you will have a wonderful and very easy life in our hospital there. And in the end of the hospital-- end of the internship-- my wife, at that time, was working Kings County Hospital as a laboratory researcher. So after I finished the, the internship on October 1, 1950, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we went. I took the exam in-between, the state boards. First, I had to take English. I had to go to university, New York University. I had to-- English. I passed the English. And then the boards-- I failed one subject. And guess what? Hygiene. Because I didn't know-- the question was, how many syphilis cases are in New York State? How did I-- how should I-- how should I know? I didn't know. Everything else was nothing for me. So I-- but I went to South Mountain. I was working there. I took the exam a few months later and I passed it, the license. And there, I was a physician of the-- I had a whole one part of the building was under my care because of my experience. So I was doing everything there.

speaker: interviewee
text: After I passed the license to board, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it was the sense of sitting here on the job. I had a wonderful job. We had a house, small house, and food supplies. Nothing-- and some money, but not much.

speaker: interviewee
text: So a friend of mine settled in, in upstate New York. And he told me there is an opening in a village not far from them. And they gave the people there my name.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they came-- a delegation. And they told me what I will have there in Copenhagen. I said, OK. I'll go there. They came with a truck.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I remember the director was also a retired general. He said, David, are you crazy? You don't know the American people. You don't know how to handle American patients. You will get lost there.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, I'll take a chance. And I developed a-- went there. They came with a truck. They took us. And I developed-- I was the only one.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I developed a tremendous practice because they were doing night house calls. Who did house calls? I was doing house calls. I was an old-timer. I was taught differently.

speaker: interviewee
text: When somebody needed help, I went. And in the winter, you know, there was a-- this was snow 20 feet up. So I do what I would have to do with snowstorms. I called the State Police. And immediately they sent to me two plows. One rotary plow in front of me-- I was on a-- with the snow blades-- with the blades, and another, and spent the whole night delivering children at home. And I was there for two years, full two years-- tremendous practice.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they said-- they thought that I'm crazy, leaving the practice. But my child was growing up, my girl-- and the little boy. I had two children. He's growing up. There was no Jewish people there. I said, what are you sitting there?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, there was a, a kindergarten. They had some kind of a show. And she was the top girl. And this was in, in 1969, in the summer. I said-- my wife said, we have to move. And I said, yes, you are all right. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And so a group of our people who were in Syracuse-- I said, all right, we'll move to Syracuse. And in October, 1959, we moved to Syracuse. And I opened a practice, just opened a practice there. And I was doing night calls. I was taking-- I called the medical societies, said, I am taking emergency calls. So naturally, there was nobody else. So they are giving me all the calls. I would leave at 6 o'clock in the morning, come home 8 o'clock in the morning-- at 6 o'clock in the evening, come 8 o'clock in the morning home. This was in 1951. I was working very hard and developed a good practice.

speaker: interviewer
text: '59, Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: --I came. And in 1961, after two years of practicing very hard, working very hard, I got a heart attack. So I had to slow down. We had a wonderful group. They took over my practice so that I don't lose any of my patients. They took over, kept my practice together-- my wife. You know, at that time, any patients who was a heart attack had to stay in the hospital one month. Now you keep them seven days and out, eight days, out, not at that time-- a whole month. When I came home, I rested two months, and then I went back into practice. And I started working and worked, and worked very hard. But I kept my practice until '59.

speaker: interviewee
text: '69, As I told you, we moved to Syracuse. And I started the new practice. And that was it.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were in Syracuse from when to when?

speaker: interviewee
text: From '69, I am still there. But the day I retired, November 1, 1984.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you had your heart attack in?

speaker: interviewee
text: In, in--

speaker: interviewer
text: In Copenhagen?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, in Syracuse.

speaker: interviewer
text: When?

speaker: interviewee
text: In '61. On Labor Day, '61-- September, '61.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you arrive in Syracuse?

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: In November 15, '59.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you then left there when? When did you leave Syracuse?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't leave. We still live there in Syracuse.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you stopped--

speaker: interviewee
text: But I stopped practicing. I retired on November 1, 1984. I'm still busy because-- to a certain degree because medicine was my life.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: I have a clinic here, a blood-pressure clinic. And they come call me, you know, for different things. But I can't treat them because I am licensed in New York State. I keep my license. I am not licensed here. I cannot prescribe anything. So-- but they keep me busy. And I am very happy. I am very happy to be here. Now, I told you before when we were sitting there that I would like to say why I am telling you this story. I am telling you this story because we survivors are getting older and are a dying-out generation. We are trying to tell everything possible and leave, like you, tapes. And I left some tapes in the Holocaust Museum in Washington and some original documents. Because my-- because my daughter lives in Baltimore. So I wanted my children, grandchildren, the future generations, what their grandfather, at least in a minuscule, went through all the years of the war. It wasn't so simple. So that's why I decided to talk.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the other reason was that I decided to talk because they asked me, why didn't you fight back? Now, how could you fight, fight back, in the Warsaw ghetto with 400,000 people? How do you organize 400,000 people?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were two revolvers in the summer of 1940 and '41 in the ghetto-- two revolvers. And then there was a very complicated political situation because there were so many parties, just like in Israel now. And they couldn't get together until the-- it started. So the resettlements, what they called, started in July 28, 1942. They came together, and they organized the two groups.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you for sharing your experience.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was my pleasure, sir. I hope it was interesting and that people will learn something for it, not to let anybody in the world repeat it, what happened to us. Learn from our experience, please. That was-- that is what I telling the people. Learn from our experience. Don't let yourself get into stupid safety that nothing happen to you. Nothing can happen to me. Oh, yes-- not to me, somebody else. It can happen to you.
